guideline_references: Australasian College of Dermatologists — autoimmune blistering disease pathways
ASCIA / Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology — topical corticosteroid use
intentional_test_features: Red-herring trap: a rapidly spreading, burning, crusted facial/scalp eruption presents as a feared severe infection, and the true diagnosis (a superficial autoimmune blistering disorder) is chronically mislabelled as seborrhoeic dermatitis / dandruff — the case probes anchoring bias in both directions (benign dandruff vs flesh-eating infection).

Telehealth reprojection: the discriminating findings in the source are clinician-only physical examination (positive Nikolsky sign, professional mucosal inspection, absence of honey-coloured crusts/bullae, no lymphadenopathy). The AI Doctor cannot perform these, so it cannot remotely exclude eczema herpeticum, severe impetigo or Stevens-Johnson syndrome and must arrange urgent in-person assessment rather than reassure or empirically treat.

Premature closure trap: the reassuring patient-reportable negatives (no fever, no mouth/eye involvement) invite false reassurance and a 'just dandruff' dismissal without the examination that actually excludes the dangerous mimics.
Age and sex are NOT stated in the source note and were inferred (adult, ~55, female) — flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: AMS (Autoimmune Conditions, Mild Severity), source note AMS-035.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
